text: The surgeon marks the left side of the abdomen for trocar placement, administers numbing medication at the belly button, and makes a small incision. The rectus sheath is opened, and the rectus muscle is retracted to the side. A trocar and balloon are inserted next to the muscle into the pelvic area where the hernia is located.

The surgeon shows the small incision made at the belly button, approximately one centimeter in size, through which a retractor is inserted. A collapsed balloon is placed through the incision and inflated, pushing the intestines and peritoneum aside to expose the muscle within the hernia. The camera is inserted, and the balloon is inflated further, working on the left side of the patient.

The surgeon removes the balloon and connects the trocar to gas insufflation, inflating the surgical area for better visibility. The rectus muscle and surrounding connective tissue are visible. Local anesthetic is administered to the muscle, followed by two small skin punctures for trocar insertion. The first trocar is placed between the rectus muscles, and the second is positioned nearby, allowing instrument access for the surgeon's left and right hands while the assistant holds the camera.

The surgeon uses a clamp to push away the vas deferens, a tube connecting the testis to the prostate. The grasper in the left hand holds the hernia sac, which is the peritoneum protruding through the muscle. The surgeon carefully teases the hernia sac back into its proper position at the bottom of the screen.
The surgeon uses a robotic grasper to carefully tease the hernia sac away from the surrounding contents that lead to the testicles, visible at the bottom of the screen.
The surgeon uses the right hand to grasp the hernia sac, which is nearly extracted from the inguinal canal, indicating that fifty to seventy percent of the surgery is complete.

The surgeon carefully dissects the hernia sac from the cord structures, including microscopic arteries, veins, and the vas deferens, using precise peeling and stripping techniques. The sac is pulled down away from the internal ring, visible at the top of the screen, ensuring minimal risk of hernia recurrence. The peritoneum is peeled off, revealing the pubic bone at the top right of the screen, while the bladder is pushed aside for clear access.

The surgeon prepares and inserts a rolled-up mesh into the surgical site, positioning it over the internal ring and the floor of the inguinal canal. Using two graspers, the mesh is unrolled and secured with dissolvable tacks near the pubic bone and the patient's side to cover the inguinal region, aiming to prevent future hernias.

The surgeon places several tacks along the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament to secure the mesh over the internal ring and the floor of the inguinal canal, completing the hernia repair. A trocar is used to evacuate gas from the abdominal cavity to minimize postoperative discomfort.
The surgeon removes the trocars and closes the muscle layer, completing the laparoscopic hernia repair. Small incisions are visible on the patient's abdomen.
